title: Reproducing Race: An Ethnography of Pregnancy as a Site of Racialization By Khiara M. Bridges University of California Press. 2011. 306 pages. $26.95 paper
abstract: Racial disparities in health outcomes have been documented since the early 19th century. Over time, the purported cause of these disparities has shifted from innate race-based biological mechanisms, to socioeconomic mechanisms, social-psychological processes, almost full circle to biogenetic differences driven by race. Khiara M. Bridges' ethnography of a women's clinic primarily serving low-income pregnant women of color offers an important rejoinder to those who would explain away disparities in health outcomes with overly deterministic theories of race as genes, as biology or as culture. Bridges provides a compelling portrait of how our political institutions shape the way that our health care institutions provide care to those who cannot afford health care at market rates, while simultaneously showing how those individuals tasked with the hands-on care of these patients—shaped by the state-driven imperatives of the health care institution as well as their own class and racial biases—work in tandem to reproduce their patients as objects of pathology. In other words, Bridges' work pushes us to examine the role that our government and health institutions play in producing the very inequalities they are charged with eliminating.
